steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The blank falls between the prepositional phrase “In a JSON file storing data about fire belly [newts]” and the transitional phrase “for instance,” which is followed by the main clause. The skill tested is punctuation: how to correctly set off the supplementary element “for instance” so the sentence remains grammatical.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — newts. For: A period after “newts” turns the opening prepositional phrase (“In a JSON file storing data about fire belly newts”) into a sentence fragment — it has no subject or verb. ✗
  • Choice B — newts, for: A comma after “newts” pairs with the comma already present after “instance,” correctly setting off “for instance” as a parenthetical supplementary element between the prepositional phrase and the main clause. ✓
  • Choice C — newts : for: A colon can introduce an elaboration but cannot be paired with a comma to bracket a supplementary phrase. Using a colon here violates the rule that both sides of a colon must function together, not as mismatched punctuation around an interrupter. ✗
  • Choice D — newts ; for: A semicolon joins two independent clauses. “In a JSON file storing data about fire belly newts” is a prepositional phrase, not an independent clause, so a semicolon is grammatically impermissible here. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
